audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel chapter 23, first-pass restored draft
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for Keilah food-system attack, repeated inquiry, followers' fear, battle and livestock, Abiathar's ephod, Saul's religious certainty, threatened siege, conditional oracle, six-hundred-person flight, Jonathan covenant, Ziphite informants, surveillance, near-encirclement, and Rock of Escape.

verses.restored: They told David, saying, Look, Philistines are fighting against Keilah and plundering threshing floors.
David inquired of YHWH, saying, Shall I go and strike these Philistines? YHWH said to David, Go, strike Philistines, and save Keilah.
Men of David said to him, Look, we are afraid here in Judah; how much more if we go to Keilah against ranks of Philistines?
David again inquired of YHWH, and YHWH answered him and said, Arise, go down to Keilah, for I am giving Philistines into your hand.
David and his men went to Keilah, fought Philistines, drove away their livestock, and struck them with great blow. David saved inhabitants of Keilah.
When Abiathar son of Ahimelech fled to David at Keilah, an ephod had come down in his hand.
It was told to Saul that David had come to Keilah. Saul said, Divine has alienated/closed him into my hand, for he has shut himself in by entering a city of doors and bar.
Saul summoned all people to war, to go down to Keilah to besiege David and his men.
David knew Saul was devising harm against him, and he said to Abiathar the priest, Bring near ephod.
David said, YHWH Divine of Israel, hearing, your servant has heard Saul seeks to come to Keilah to destroy city because of me.
Will leaders of Keilah deliver me into his hand? Will Saul come down as your servant has heard? YHWH Divine of Israel, tell your servant, please. YHWH said, He will come down.
David said, Will leaders of Keilah deliver me and my men into hand of Saul? YHWH said, They will deliver.
David arose with his men, about six hundred, and they went out from Keilah and moved wherever they could. It was told to Saul David escaped from Keilah, and he ceased going out.
David stayed in wilderness in strongholds and stayed in hill country in wilderness of Ziph. Saul sought him all days, but Divine did not give him into his hand.
David saw Saul had gone out to seek his life. David was in wilderness of Ziph at Horesh.
Jonathan son of Saul arose and went to David at Horesh and strengthened his hand in Divine.
He said to him, Do not fear, for hand of Saul my father shall not find you. You will reign over Israel, and I shall be second to you. Saul my father also knows this.
The two cut covenant before YHWH. David stayed at Horesh, and Jonathan went to his house.
Ziphites went up to Saul at Gibeah, saying, Is not David hiding with us in strongholds at Horesh, on hill of Hachilah south of Jeshimon?
Now according to all desire of your life, king, come down; ours is to deliver him into hand of king.
Saul said, Blessed are you of YHWH, because you have had compassion on me.
Go, please, make yet more certain. Know and see his place, where his foot is, who has seen him there, for it is said to me he is very cunning.
See and know all hiding places where he hides. Return to me with certainty, and I will go with you. If he is in land, I will search him among all thousands/clans of Judah.
They arose and went to Ziph before Saul. David and his men were in wilderness of Maon, in Arabah south of Jeshimon.
Saul and his men went to seek, and it was told to David. He went down to rock and stayed in wilderness of Maon. Saul heard and pursued David in wilderness of Maon.
Saul went on one side of mountain, and David and his men on other side. David was hurrying to go away from before Saul, while Saul and his men were surrounding David and his men to seize them.
A messenger came to Saul, saying, Hurry and come, for Philistines have made a raid upon land.
Saul returned from pursuing David and went to meet Philistines. Therefore they called that place Rock of Divisions/Escape.
David went up from there and stayed in strongholds of En-gedi.

translation_notes: Keilah faces economic attack at threshing floors. Food systems and civilians are central to the crisis, not merely military territory.
David's men openly name fear. A leader's repeated discernment should include the fear, consent, and capacity of vulnerable followers rather than shame them.
The rescue includes battlefield killing and seizure of livestock. Saving a city does not make all wartime actions beyond moral examination.
Saul interprets David's location as Divine delivery while planning siege and city destruction. Religious certainty can be profoundly wrong when joined to coercive power.
The oracle about Keilah's leaders describes a likely future under pressure, not a moral license to condemn the whole city as traitorous.
David leaves rather than make Keilah absorb a siege on his behalf. Refugee movements may protect both fugitives and host communities.
Jonathan strengthens David and acknowledges a future he believes will displace his own dynasty. Their covenant remains intimate and political.
The Ziphites' cooperation with Saul may reflect local fear, politics, or survival; the text does not justify hatred of a population or place.
Saul blesses informants in YHWH's name while hunting David. Divine language does not validate surveillance, informant systems, or persecution.
The near-encirclement and sudden Philistine raid show contingency rather than a simple triumphal rescue formula.
Names such as Rock of Escape preserve survival memory; they do not prove that all later political claims attached to a place are Divinely guaranteed.
safety_note: Do not shame David's men for fear, condemn all Keilahites or Ziphites as betrayers, or use Divine guidance language to justify surveillance, siege, informant networks, or political pursuit. Saul sincerely invokes YHWH while planning harm, demonstrating that religious certainty is not moral proof. Refugees may leave a host community to prevent collective punishment, and communities under pressure should not be judged as though they possess unlimited freedom.
